questions.q: What is a Component in Angular?

answer.oneLiner: A Component is the basic UI building block — a TypeScript class with @Component that bundles a template, styles, and logic into a reusable unit.
keyPoints: Decorated with `@Component({ selector, template, styles })`
Selector lets you use it as a custom HTML element
Can be standalone (v14+) or part of an NgModule
Components form a tree rooted at AppComponent

code.note: Minimal standalone component with an @Input binding

answer.analogy: Like a LEGO brick — shape (template), colour (styles), connection points (@Input/@Output).
redFlags: Confusing a component with a directive
Not knowing what the selector is for
Assuming it works exactly like a React function component

dialogue.text: So, in your own words, what is a component in Angular?
It's the basic UI building block: a TypeScript class decorated with `@Component` that bundles a template, styles, and logic into one reusable unit.
What does the selector actually give you?
It registers a custom HTML tag, so I can drop the component into another template as `<app-greeting>`.
How do components relate to each other in an app?
They form a tree rooted at the bootstrapped `AppComponent`, with data flowing down via inputs and events bubbling up via outputs.

followUpsQA.q: Standalone component vs one declared in an NgModule?
followUpsQA.a: Standalone declares its own imports in the decorator; NgModule-based components are declared and wired up in a module.

followUpsQA.q: How does Angular pick the component for a route?
followUpsQA.a: The router matches the URL to a route config and renders its `component` (or lazily `loadComponent`) in the router outlet.

followUpsQA.q: How would you share state between siblings?
followUpsQA.a: Lift it into a shared service (often a BehaviorSubject or signal) that both siblings inject.

followUpsQA.q: Is a component a directive?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, a component is technically a directive with a template.

questions.q: What are Directives and what is the difference between structural and attribute directives?

answer.oneLiner: Directives add behaviour to elements. Structural directives change the DOM layout (add/remove nodes); attribute directives change the look or behaviour of an existing element.
keyPoints: Structural: `*ngIf`, `*ngFor` — the `*` is sugar for `<ng-template>`
Attribute: `ngClass`, `ngStyle`, custom directives — no DOM add/remove
Components are technically a directive with a template
Angular 17+ adds `@if` / `@for` / `@switch` control flow blocks
answer.detail: Only one structural directive per element — use `<ng-container>` to apply several. The new control flow blocks are the preferred, boilerplate-free alternative.

code.snippet:
Welcome back!

Please log in.

 @if (isLoggedIn) {
Welcome back!
} @else {

Please log in.

}

Status
code.note: Old *ngIf vs Angular 17 @if control flow, plus ngClass attribute directive

redFlags: Saying *ngIf only hides elements (it removes from DOM)
Not knowing `*` expands to `<ng-template>`
Unaware of Angular 17 `@if` / `@for`

dialogue.text: What's the difference between structural and attribute directives?
Structural directives change the DOM layout by adding or removing nodes, like `*ngIf` and `*ngFor`; attribute directives just change the look or behaviour of an existing element, like `ngClass`.
What does that asterisk in `*ngIf` really mean?
It's syntactic sugar that wraps the element in an `<ng-template>` so Angular can stamp it in or out.
Is there a newer way to do this in Angular 17?
Yes, the built-in control flow blocks `@if`, `@for`, and `@switch` replace the structural directives with less boilerplate.

followUpsQA.q: How would you build a custom structural directive?
followUpsQA.a: Inject `TemplateRef` and `ViewContainerRef`, then create or clear the embedded view based on your input.

followUpsQA.q: What is ng-container and why is it useful?
followUpsQA.a: A logical grouping element that renders nothing, so you can apply structural directives without adding an extra DOM node.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between ngClass and [class.active]?
followUpsQA.a: `[class.active]` toggles a single class; `ngClass` takes an object or string for multiple classes at once.

followUpsQA.q: Can you put two structural directives on one element?
followUpsQA.a: No, only one per element; use `<ng-container>` or nest them.

questions.q: What does the Angular compiler do? Explain AOT vs JIT and the role of Ivy.

answer.oneLiner: The compiler turns templates and decorators into optimised JS. AOT compiles at build time (the production default), JIT compiles in the browser, and Ivy is the current compile/render engine.
keyPoints: JIT: compiler shipped to browser, compiles at runtime — dev only
AOT: compiled at build, smaller bundles, catches template errors early
Ivy is the engine (default since v9), replacing View Engine
Ivy enables tree-shaking, locality, standalone, and signals
answer.detail: AOT is the default for both `ng build` and `ng serve` since v12. Ivy compiles each component independently (locality), which speeds up incremental builds and improves tree-shaking.

code.note: AOT is on by default; Ivy factories are what the Angular runtime executes

redFlags: Thinking JIT is used in production
Confusing Ivy with a specific Angular version
Not knowing AOT catches template errors at build

dialogue.text: What does the Angular compiler do, and what's AOT versus JIT?
It turns templates and decorators into optimised JavaScript. AOT compiles at build time and is the production default; JIT compiles in the browser and is really just for dev now.
Why prefer AOT for production?
Smaller bundles since the compiler isn't shipped, faster startup, and it catches template errors at build time instead of runtime.
Where does Ivy fit in?
Ivy is the current compile and render engine, default since v9; it compiles each component independently, which improves tree-shaking and enables standalone and signals.

followUpsQA.q: Benefits of strictTemplates?
followUpsQA.a: It type-checks bindings in templates, catching mismatched inputs and typos at build time.

followUpsQA.q: How does Ivy locality help large monorepo builds?
followUpsQA.a: Each component compiles to a self-contained factory, so incremental rebuilds only touch what changed.

followUpsQA.q: Is differential loading still relevant in Angular 17+?
followUpsQA.a: No, modern browsers are targeted directly so differential loading was dropped.

followUpsQA.q: Is JIT ever used in production?
followUpsQA.a: No, production uses AOT; JIT is dev-only.

questions.q: Describe Angular's component lifecycle hooks and when each fires.

answer.oneLiner: Components run through hooks from creation to destruction — the key ones are ngOnChanges, ngOnInit, ngAfterViewInit, and ngOnDestroy.
keyPoints: `ngOnChanges` — on every @Input change, before ngOnInit
`ngOnInit` — once after first inputs set; fetch initial data here
`ngAfterViewInit` — view ready; safe to access @ViewChild
`ngOnDestroy` — cleanup; unsubscribe and clear timers
answer.detail: Order: constructor, ngOnChanges, ngOnInit, ngDoCheck, ngAfterContentInit/Checked, ngAfterViewInit/Checked, ngOnDestroy. @ViewChild is undefined in ngOnInit. With signals you can use `effect()` instead of ngDoCheck.

code.note: Pattern: takeUntil + destroy$ Subject to auto-unsubscribe on component destruction

redFlags: Putting HTTP calls in the constructor
Accessing @ViewChild in ngOnInit
Not cleaning up subscriptions in ngOnDestroy

dialogue.text: Walk me through the main lifecycle hooks.
`ngOnChanges` fires on every input change, `ngOnInit` runs once after the first inputs are set, `ngAfterViewInit` when the view is ready, and `ngOnDestroy` for cleanup.
Where would you put your initial data fetch?
In `ngOnInit`, because inputs are guaranteed to be set by then and it only runs once.
And what's the one thing people forget in ngOnDestroy?
Unsubscribing from observables and clearing timers, otherwise you leak memory; I usually use `takeUntilDestroyed` or a `destroy$` subject.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between ngOnInit and the constructor?
followUpsQA.a: The constructor is for DI and runs before inputs are set; ngOnInit runs after the first inputs are bound.

followUpsQA.q: Why can't you access @ViewChild in ngOnInit?
followUpsQA.a: The view isn't rendered yet, so the query resolves only by ngAfterViewInit.

followUpsQA.q: What does takeUntilDestroyed() do?
followUpsQA.a: It auto-completes a subscription when the component is destroyed, using the injection context's DestroyRef.

followUpsQA.q: When does ngOnChanges fire relative to ngOnInit?
followUpsQA.a: Before the first ngOnInit, and again on every subsequent input change.

questions.q: What type of DOM does Angular use, and how does it compare to React's virtual DOM?

answer.oneLiner: Angular uses the real DOM with an incremental DOM strategy via Ivy; React uses a virtual DOM that it diffs and patches. Angular's approach is more memory efficient since there's no shadow tree.
keyPoints: Angular works on the real DOM — no virtual DOM
Ivy compiles templates to instructions that update only changed nodes
React diffs an in-memory VDOM tree, then patches the real DOM
Incremental DOM uses less memory and tree-shakes better
answer.detail: Ivy's instructions live alongside the component, so no intermediate tree is built. Change detection (Zone.js or signals) decides when to run; Ivy instructions decide how. Both perform well in practice.
answer.analogy: VDOM is redrawing the whole blueprint then comparing; incremental DOM is a checklist that only touches the listed items.
redFlags: Claiming Angular uses a virtual DOM
Not knowing what Ivy is
Saying one approach is always faster

dialogue.text: Does Angular use a virtual DOM like React?
No, Angular works on the real DOM with an incremental DOM strategy through Ivy; there's no in-memory shadow tree.
So how does it know what to update?
Ivy compiles templates into instructions that update only the nodes that actually changed, rather than diffing a whole tree.
What's the practical advantage versus a virtual DOM?
Lower memory because there's no intermediate tree to build, and it tree-shakes better; though in practice both approaches perform well.

followUpsQA.q: How does OnPush reduce DOM work?
followUpsQA.a: It skips change detection for a component unless its input reference changes, an event fires, or an async pipe emits.

followUpsQA.q: How do signals eliminate Zone.js overhead?
followUpsQA.a: Signals notify only their dependents on change, so Angular can update targeted views without Zone.js scanning the whole tree.

followUpsQA.q: What is hydration in Angular SSR?
followUpsQA.a: It reuses server-rendered DOM on the client instead of re-rendering, avoiding flicker and improving startup.

followUpsQA.q: Is incremental DOM always faster than VDOM?
followUpsQA.a: No, both are fast in practice; the main win is memory and tree-shaking.

questions.q: What architecture does Angular follow?

answer.oneLiner: A component-based architecture with built-in dependency injection, loosely MVVM — components are the ViewModel binding templates (View) to services (Model).
keyPoints: Component tree rooted at AppComponent
Hierarchical DI for services
MVVM-ish: component = ViewModel, template = View, services = Model
Full opinionated framework: routing, forms, HTTP built in
answer.detail: NgModules grouped components historically but are being superseded by standalone components (default v17+). A service in `root` is a singleton; provided in a component it's scoped to that subtree.
redFlags: Calling Angular a library
Not knowing what DI is in Angular
Confusing it with React's data flow model

dialogue.text: What architecture does Angular follow?
A component-based architecture with built-in hierarchical dependency injection, and it's loosely MVVM.
How does it map to MVVM?
The component is the ViewModel, the template is the View, and services hold the Model or business logic.
Library or framework?
A full opinionated framework: routing, forms, and HTTP all come built in, unlike a library where you assemble those yourself.

followUpsQA.q: How does Angular DI differ from manual instantiation?
followUpsQA.a: DI provides and injects dependencies for you, so classes are decoupled and easy to mock in tests.

followUpsQA.q: Advantages of standalone over NgModules?
followUpsQA.a: Less boilerplate, clearer dependencies, and better lazy-loading ergonomics.

followUpsQA.q: How would you structure a large Angular app?
followUpsQA.a: By feature folders with lazy-loaded routes, shared and core layers, and smart/dumb component separation.

followUpsQA.q: Is a root-provided service a singleton?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, `providedIn: 'root'` gives one app-wide instance.

questions.q: What is lazy loading in Angular and what are its advantages?

answer.oneLiner: Lazy loading defers downloading a route's code until the user navigates to it, shrinking the initial bundle and speeding up startup.
keyPoints: Use `loadComponent` (standalone) or `loadChildren` in routes
CLI auto code-splits each lazy route into its own chunk
Reduces initial bundle and improves Time to Interactive
Combine with preloading strategies for background loading
answer.detail: Lazy routes get their own injector scope, so services provided there don't leak. Preloading options: `NoPreloading` (default), `PreloadAllModules`, or a custom strategy.

code.note: loadComponent for a single component, loadChildren for a sub-route array

redFlags: Thinking lazy loading hides routes from users
Not knowing `loadComponent` is the modern way
Confusing it with ngIf show/hide

dialogue.text: What is lazy loading and why use it?
It defers downloading a route's code until the user navigates there, which shrinks the initial bundle and speeds up startup.
How do you set it up with standalone components?
In the route I use `loadComponent` for a single component or `loadChildren` for a sub-route array, returning a dynamic `import()`.
Any side effect on dependency injection?
Lazy routes get their own injector scope, so services provided there are isolated and don't leak into the rest of the app.

followUpsQA.q: How do you implement a custom preloading strategy?
followUpsQA.a: Extend `PreloadingStrategy` and decide per-route whether to preload, then pass it to `provideRouter(withPreloading(...))`.

followUpsQA.q: loadChildren with a module vs a route array?
followUpsQA.a: Modern standalone apps return a route array; legacy apps return an NgModule.

followUpsQA.q: How does lazy loading interact with DI scoping?
followUpsQA.a: Each lazy route creates an environment injector, scoping its providers to that route subtree.

followUpsQA.q: Does lazy loading hide routes from users?
followUpsQA.a: No, it only defers code download; it's not a security feature.

questions.q: What are standalone components and how do they differ from NgModule-based components?

answer.oneLiner: Standalone components declare their own imports directly in @Component instead of belonging to an NgModule, removing the NgModule boilerplate layer.
keyPoints: `standalone: true` (default in Angular 17+)
Component lists its own `imports: [...]`
No @NgModule declaration needed
Bootstrapped with `bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, ...)`
answer.detail: Standalone components can still import legacy NgModules like `ReactiveFormsModule`. Migrate with `ng generate @angular/core:standalone`.

code.note: Standalone apps use bootstrapApplication instead of platformBrowserDynamic().bootstrapModule()

redFlags: Not knowing standalone is the default in v17
Thinking standalone can't use NgModule libraries
Confusing standalone with micro-frontends

dialogue.text: What are standalone components?
Components that declare their own dependencies in `imports` directly in `@Component`, instead of belonging to an NgModule.
How do you bootstrap a standalone app?
With `bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, { providers: [...] })` in main.ts, no root NgModule needed.
Can standalone components still use older NgModule libraries?
Yes, you just add the NgModule, like `ReactiveFormsModule`, to the component's `imports` array.

followUpsQA.q: How do you provide a service to just a standalone subtree?
followUpsQA.a: Add it to the component's `providers` array or use route-level providers.

followUpsQA.q: Can a standalone component use an NgModule directive?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, import the NgModule that exports it into the component's imports.

followUpsQA.q: Why use provideRouter instead of RouterModule.forRoot?
followUpsQA.a: It's the tree-shakable, standalone-friendly API and composes with features like `withPreloading`.

followUpsQA.q: Is standalone the default now?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, it's the default since Angular 17.

questions.q: What is an Angular Service and how does Dependency Injection work?

answer.oneLiner: A Service is a reusable @Injectable class for logic, HTTP, or shared state. Angular's DI injects it via the constructor or the inject() function.
keyPoints: `@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })` = app-wide singleton
Inject via constructor or `inject()`
Hierarchical injector — provide in a component to scope it
DI enables testability via mock injection
answer.detail: Injector hierarchy: platform > root > environment (lazy scopes) > component. Provide in `@Component.providers` to scope a service to that component tree.

code.note: Modern Angular uses inject() function instead of constructor injection — works outside of constructors

redFlags: Not knowing what `providedIn: 'root'` means
Creating services with `new` instead of injecting
Not seeing how DI enables testing

dialogue.text: What's a service and how does DI work?
A service is a reusable `@Injectable` class for logic, HTTP, or shared state, and Angular's DI injects it via the constructor or the `inject()` function.
What does providedIn: 'root' mean?
It registers the service as an app-wide singleton and makes it tree-shakable if nothing uses it.
How would you scope a service to just one component tree?
Provide it in that component's `providers` array, so the hierarchical injector creates a fresh instance per component.

followUpsQA.q: inject() function vs constructor injection?
followUpsQA.a: `inject()` works anywhere in an injection context, including field initializers and functions, not just constructors.

followUpsQA.q: How to provide different impls per environment?
followUpsQA.a: Use an injection token with `useClass`/`useFactory` and swap the provider per environment config.

followUpsQA.q: What is a multi-provider token?
followUpsQA.a: A token registered with `multi: true` that collects multiple providers into an array, like interceptors.

followUpsQA.q: How does DI enable testing?
followUpsQA.a: You can inject mock implementations in place of real services without changing the consumer.

questions.q: What is the Angular CLI and what are its most important commands?

answer.oneLiner: The CLI scaffolds, builds, serves, tests, and lints Angular apps with no manual build config.
keyPoints: `ng new` — scaffold a project; `ng serve` — dev server
`ng generate` (`ng g c`) — scaffold components/services/etc.
`ng build --configuration=production` — AOT, minified bundle
`ng test`, `ng e2e`, `ng update` — test and safe migrations
answer.detail: The CLI abstracts webpack and uses esbuild by default since v17 (the `application` builder) for much faster builds. `ng add` runs a package's schematic to install and configure it.
redFlags: Not knowing the basic generate commands
Editing webpack config directly
Confusing `ng update` with `npm update`

dialogue.text: What is the Angular CLI and what do you use it for?
It scaffolds, builds, serves, tests, and lints Angular apps without manual build config; the day-to-day commands are `ng new`, `ng serve`, and `ng generate`.
What does ng generate save you from?
Hand-writing boilerplate; `ng g c` creates a component with its class, template, styles, and wiring in one go.
What changed in builds recently?
Since v17 the CLI uses the esbuild-based `application` builder by default, so production builds are much faster than the old webpack pipeline.

followUpsQA.q: How to configure environment-specific builds?
followUpsQA.a: Use build configurations in angular.json with file replacements per environment.

followUpsQA.q: ng build vs ng build --configuration production?
followUpsQA.a: The production configuration enables AOT, minification, and budgets for an optimised bundle.

followUpsQA.q: What is the v17 application builder?
followUpsQA.a: An esbuild/Vite-based builder that replaces the webpack browser builder for faster builds and dev server.

followUpsQA.q: What does ng add do?
followUpsQA.a: It runs a library's schematic to install and configure it in one step.

questions.q: What are smart (container) and dumb (presentational) components?

answer.oneLiner: Smart (container) components manage state and services; dumb (presentational) components just take @Input and emit @Output with no service dependencies.
keyPoints: Smart: inject services, orchestrate children
Dumb: pure UI driven by inputs/outputs
Dumb components pair well with OnPush
Improves testability and reusability
answer.detail: It's a guideline, not a rule — some components legitimately need both. Maps to flux/redux: smart = connected, dumb = presentational.
redFlags: Not knowing the pattern for a senior role
Thinking 'dumb' means bad
Injecting services into presentational components

dialogue.text: Explain smart versus dumb components.
Smart or container components manage state and inject services; dumb or presentational components just take inputs and emit outputs with no service dependencies.
Why bother with the split?
It improves reusability and testability, since dumb components are pure UI you can test with just inputs and outputs.
How does it pair with change detection?
Dumb components work great with OnPush because they're driven entirely by input references.

followUpsQA.q: When is it OK to inject a service in a dumb component?
followUpsQA.a: For purely presentational concerns like a translation pipe; avoid injecting state or data services.

followUpsQA.q: How does this interact with NgRx or a signal store?
followUpsQA.a: Smart components select state and dispatch; dumb components stay store-agnostic.

followUpsQA.q: How does OnPush benefit dumb components?
followUpsQA.a: With immutable inputs they only re-render when a reference changes, cutting CD work.

followUpsQA.q: Is the split a hard rule?
followUpsQA.a: No, it's a guideline; some components legitimately need both roles.

questions.q: What are ViewChild and ContentChild decorators?

answer.oneLiner: @ViewChild queries the component's own template; @ContentChild queries projected content (ng-content). Each is ready only after its AfterInit hook.
keyPoints: @ViewChild — first match in own template
@ContentChild — content projected via `<ng-content>`
Ready in ngAfterViewInit / ngAfterContentInit (not ngOnInit)
@ViewChildren / @ContentChildren return a QueryList
answer.detail: `{ static: true }` makes a query available in ngOnInit but only for elements always in the DOM. Signal-based `viewChild()` / `contentChild()` (v17.1+) give reactive access without lifecycle coupling.

code.note: viewChild() signal function — reactive alternative to @ViewChild decorator

redFlags: Accessing @ViewChild in the constructor/ngOnInit without static
Confusing ViewChild with ContentChild
Not knowing ViewChildren returns a QueryList

dialogue.text: What's the difference between ViewChild and ContentChild?
`@ViewChild` queries the component's own template, while `@ContentChild` queries content projected in through `<ng-content>`.
When are those queries actually available?
In their AfterInit hooks: `ngAfterViewInit` for ViewChild and `ngAfterContentInit` for ContentChild, not in `ngOnInit`.
Any modern alternative?
Yes, the signal-based `viewChild()` and `contentChild()` functions from v17.1 give reactive access without coupling to lifecycle hooks.

followUpsQA.q: @ViewChild vs @ViewChildren?
followUpsQA.a: ViewChild returns the first match; ViewChildren returns a QueryList of all matches.

followUpsQA.q: Why is @ViewChild undefined in ngOnInit?
followUpsQA.a: The view hasn't rendered yet, so the query resolves only by ngAfterViewInit.

followUpsQA.q: How do signal-based viewChild()/contentChild() improve things?
followUpsQA.a: They're reactive and available without waiting on a specific lifecycle hook.

followUpsQA.q: What does { static: true } do?
followUpsQA.a: It resolves the query before ngOnInit, but only for elements always present in the DOM.

questions.q: What are the four types of data binding in Angular?

answer.oneLiner: Interpolation {{ }}, property binding [prop], event binding (event), and two-way binding [(ngModel)] which is just property + event combined.
keyPoints: Interpolation `{{ value }}` — value as text
Property `[prop]="expr"` — sets a DOM property or @Input
Event `(event)="handler($event)"` — DOM events or @Output
Two-way `[(ngModel)]` — needs FormsModule
answer.detail: Property binding targets DOM properties, not HTML attributes — use `[attr.x]` for attributes. Banana-in-a-box `[()]` = input down + output up; pair `@Input() x` with `@Output() xChange` for custom two-way.

code.snippet: {{ title }}
code.note: Each binding type and its syntax at a glance

redFlags: Confusing HTML attributes with DOM properties
Using `[(ngModel)]` in reactive forms
Forgetting to import FormsModule

dialogue.text: What are the four types of data binding?
Interpolation with `{{ }}`, property binding with `[prop]`, event binding with `(event)`, and two-way binding with `[(ngModel)]`.
What is two-way binding really doing under the hood?
It's just property binding down plus event binding up combined; the banana-in-a-box is `[prop]` paired with `(propChange)`.
Does property binding set attributes or DOM properties?
DOM properties; for actual HTML attributes I use `[attr.x]`, for example `[attr.colspan]`.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between [src] and src={{ }}?
followUpsQA.a: `[src]` is property binding to an expression; `src="{{ }}"` interpolates into the attribute string, fine for simple cases but not for non-string values.

followUpsQA.q: How to bind an HTML attribute vs a DOM property?
followUpsQA.a: Use `[attr.name]` for attributes and `[property]` for DOM properties.

followUpsQA.q: How does two-way binding work without ngModel?
followUpsQA.a: Pair an `@Input() x` with an `@Output() xChange` and the `[(x)]` syntax works.

followUpsQA.q: What module does [(ngModel)] need?
followUpsQA.a: `FormsModule`.

questions.q: What is the difference between a Promise and an Observable?

answer.oneLiner: A Promise is eager, resolves once, and can't be cancelled. An Observable is lazy, cancellable, can emit many values, and composes via RxJS operators.
keyPoints: Promise: eager, single value, not cancellable
Observable: lazy, 0–N values, cancellable via unsubscribe
Observables have operators (map, switchMap, etc.)
Angular HTTP returns Observables; async pipe auto-manages them
answer.detail: Bridge with `firstValueFrom()`/`lastValueFrom()` (Observable to Promise) and `from()` (Promise to Observable). Prefer Observables for HTTP, streams, and complex async pipelines.
redFlags: Saying Observables are just fancier Promises
Not knowing Observables are lazy
Not knowing how to cancel an HTTP request

dialogue.text: Promise versus Observable, what's the difference?
A Promise is eager, resolves once, and can't be cancelled; an Observable is lazy, cancellable, can emit many values, and composes with RxJS operators.
Why does HttpClient return an Observable for a single response?
So you get cancellation via unsubscribe, retry and other operators, and it plays nicely with the async pipe.
How do you bridge to a Promise when you need one?
With `firstValueFrom()` or `lastValueFrom()`, since `toPromise()` is deprecated.

followUpsQA.q: Why does HttpClient return an Observable?
followUpsQA.a: For cancellation, operator composition, and async-pipe integration.

followUpsQA.q: How do you convert an Observable to a Promise?
followUpsQA.a: Use `firstValueFrom()` or `lastValueFrom()`.

followUpsQA.q: firstValueFrom vs deprecated toPromise?
followUpsQA.a: `firstValueFrom` resolves on the first emission and rejects on empty; `toPromise` is removed and resolved only on completion.

followUpsQA.q: Are Observables just fancier Promises?
followUpsQA.a: No, they're lazy, multi-value, and cancellable, which Promises aren't.

questions.q: What are @Input and @Output and how do they enable component communication?

answer.oneLiner: @Input passes data down from parent to child; @Output emits events up from child to parent via EventEmitter.
keyPoints: `@Input()` — parent binds `[prop]="value"` (data down)
`@Output()` EventEmitter — parent listens `(event)="..."` (events up)
Inputs support `required` and `transform` (v16+)
Signal `input()` / `output()` (v17.1+) are reactive alternatives
answer.detail: For non parent-child communication, use a shared service or state store. Signal inputs are readable in computed/effect without ngOnChanges.

code.note: Signal inputs are reactive — usable in computed() and effect() without ngOnChanges

redFlags: Mutating an @Input directly in the child
Not knowing @Output uses EventEmitter
Not knowing signal input/output for a senior role

dialogue.text: How do @Input and @Output enable communication?
`@Input` passes data down from parent to child, and `@Output` emits events up from child to parent via an EventEmitter.
What if two components aren't in a parent-child relationship?
Then I use a shared service or a state store rather than inputs and outputs.
Any modern reactive flavour of these?
Yes, signal `input()` and `output()` from v17.1; the input is readable in `computed` and `effect` without needing `ngOnChanges`.

followUpsQA.q: How do you alias an @Input?
followUpsQA.a: Pass an alias string, like `@Input('userId') id`, or `input(..., { alias: 'userId' })`.

followUpsQA.q: How do signal inputs affect change detection?
followUpsQA.a: They integrate with fine-grained reactivity, so reads track dependencies and trigger targeted updates.

followUpsQA.q: How to pass data between sibling components?
followUpsQA.a: Through a shared service or store both siblings inject.

followUpsQA.q: Should a child mutate an @Input directly?
followUpsQA.a: No, emit a change via @Output and let the parent own the data.

questions.q: What is the difference between Reactive Forms and Template-driven Forms? When should you use each?

answer.oneLiner: Reactive forms define the model in TypeScript (FormGroup/FormControl) for full control and testability; template-driven forms use ngModel in the template and suit simple cases.
keyPoints: Reactive: model in class, `ReactiveFormsModule`, FormGroup/Array
Template-driven: model from ngModel, `FormsModule`, simpler
Reactive is easier to test and supports dynamic fields
Reactive forms are typed since Angular 14+
answer.detail: Use reactive for complex, dynamic, or cross-field-validated forms; template-driven for quick simple forms. Don't mix ngModel and formControl on one element.
redFlags: Mixing ngModel and formControl on one element
Not knowing how to read validation errors
Choosing template-driven for a dynamic wizard

dialogue.text: Reactive forms versus template-driven, when do you use each?
Reactive forms define the model in TypeScript with `FormGroup` and `FormControl` for full control; template-driven forms use `ngModel` in the template and suit simple cases.
Which would you pick for a dynamic multi-step wizard?
Reactive, because I can build controls dynamically, run cross-field validation, and unit-test the form model easily.
Any recent improvement to reactive forms?
They've been strictly typed since Angular 14, so control values and form state carry real types.

followUpsQA.q: How do you do cross-field validation in reactive forms?
followUpsQA.a: Add a validator at the FormGroup level that compares sibling controls.

followUpsQA.q: What is FormArray and when to use it?
followUpsQA.a: A dynamic list of controls, ideal for repeating fields like multiple phone numbers.

followUpsQA.q: How do typed reactive forms help?
followUpsQA.a: They catch type mismatches at compile time and improve editor autocomplete.

followUpsQA.q: Can you mix ngModel and formControl on one element?
followUpsQA.a: No, that's an anti-pattern and will throw.

questions.q: What is RxJS? Explain Observables, operators, and subscriptions.

answer.oneLiner: RxJS is a reactive library built on Observables — lazy, composable, cancellable streams — with operators like map, filter, and switchMap to transform and combine them.
keyPoints: Observable: lazy stream; runs only on subscribe
Subscription: the execution; unsubscribe to cancel
Operators chain via `.pipe()` to transform streams
Angular uses RxJS in HttpClient, Router, valueChanges
answer.detail: A Subject is both Observable and Observer and can multicast. Prefer the `async` pipe over manual subscriptions to avoid leaks.

code.snippet:
searchResults$ = this.searchControl.valueChanges.pipe(
  debounceTime(300),           // wait 300ms after last keystroke
  distinctUntilChanged(),      // ignore if same as last value
  filter(q => q.length > 2),   // minimum 3 chars
  switchMap(q => this.api.search(q)), // cancel previous request
  catchError(err => of([]))    // handle errors gracefully
);
code.note: Typical type-ahead pipeline with debounce, distinct, and switchMap

redFlags: Nested subscriptions instead of flattening operators
Not unsubscribing from infinite Observables
Misusing switchMap when concatMap is needed

dialogue.text: What is RxJS and what are Observables, operators, and subscriptions?
RxJS is a reactive library built on Observables, which are lazy, composable, cancellable streams; operators transform them via `.pipe()`, and a subscription is the actual execution.
Where does Angular use RxJS for me automatically?
In HttpClient, the Router events, and form `valueChanges`, among others.
How do you avoid leaking subscriptions in templates?
I prefer the async pipe, which subscribes and unsubscribes for me.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between map and switchMap?
followUpsQA.a: `map` transforms a value synchronously; `switchMap` maps to an inner observable and flattens it, cancelling the previous.

followUpsQA.q: combineLatest vs forkJoin?
followUpsQA.a: `combineLatest` emits on every source change once all have emitted; `forkJoin` emits once when all complete.

followUpsQA.q: How to handle errors without killing the stream?
followUpsQA.a: Use `catchError` inside the inner observable so the outer stream survives.

followUpsQA.q: What makes a Subject special?
followUpsQA.a: It's both an Observable and an Observer, so it can multicast.

questions.q: How do you prevent memory leaks from RxJS subscriptions in Angular?

answer.oneLiner: Use the async pipe (auto-unsubscribes), takeUntilDestroyed() (v16+), or a destroy$ Subject with takeUntil.
keyPoints: `async` pipe — auto subscribe/unsubscribe in templates
`takeUntilDestroyed()` (v16+) — cleanest imperative way
`takeUntil(destroy$)` — classic Subject + ngOnDestroy
Finite Observables (take(1), HTTP) complete on their own
answer.detail: `DestroyRef.onDestroy()` covers non-component contexts. HTTP completes after one emission, but switchMap/interval pipelines still need cleanup.

code.note: takeUntilDestroyed with an injected DestroyRef — the modern pattern

redFlags: Not knowing subscription leaks exist
Only knowing the destroy$ pattern, not takeUntilDestroyed
Thinking all HttpClient subscriptions always complete

dialogue.text: How do you prevent memory leaks from RxJS subscriptions?
The cleanest options are the async pipe, which auto-unsubscribes, or `takeUntilDestroyed()` from v16 for imperative subscriptions.
What about before takeUntilDestroyed existed?
The classic pattern is a `destroy$` Subject with `takeUntil(destroy$)`, completed in `ngOnDestroy`.
Do you always need to unsubscribe?
No, finite streams like HTTP or `take(1)` complete on their own; it's the long-lived ones like `interval` or `switchMap` pipelines that need cleanup.

followUpsQA.q: What is the DestroyRef token?
followUpsQA.a: An injectable that lets you register cleanup callbacks tied to the owner's destruction.

followUpsQA.q: How does toSignal() handle cleanup?
followUpsQA.a: It unsubscribes automatically when its injection context is destroyed.

followUpsQA.q: Are HttpClient subscriptions safe to leave?
followUpsQA.a: Mostly, since they complete after one emission, but a never-resolving request could still hang.

followUpsQA.q: Why prefer the async pipe?
followUpsQA.a: It manages subscription lifecycle in the template, so you can't forget to unsubscribe.

questions.q: What is the difference between Subject, BehaviorSubject, ReplaySubject, and AsyncSubject?

answer.oneLiner: Subject is a basic multicast; BehaviorSubject holds a current value for late subscribers; ReplaySubject replays N past values; AsyncSubject emits only the last value on complete.
keyPoints: Subject — no initial value, late subscribers miss past emissions
BehaviorSubject — has current value via `.value`, needs an initial
ReplaySubject(n) — replays last n to new subscribers
AsyncSubject — emits only the final value on complete
answer.detail: BehaviorSubject is the common choice for shared state in services. Expose it via `asObservable()` so consumers can't call `.next()`.

code.note: asObservable() prevents consumers from calling .next() directly

redFlags: Not knowing BehaviorSubject needs an initial value
Exposing the Subject itself so consumers can call .next()
Confusing Subject with BehaviorSubject

dialogue.text: Compare Subject, BehaviorSubject, ReplaySubject, and AsyncSubject.
Subject is a plain multicast, BehaviorSubject holds a current value for late subscribers, ReplaySubject replays the last N, and AsyncSubject emits only the final value on complete.
Which would you use for shared state in a service?
Usually a BehaviorSubject, since new subscribers immediately get the current value and I can read `.value` synchronously.
How do you protect that state from outside mutation?
Expose it as `asObservable()` so consumers can subscribe but can't call `.next()`.

followUpsQA.q: Why expose BehaviorSubject as asObservable()?
followUpsQA.a: To prevent consumers from pushing values and to keep the service the single writer.

followUpsQA.q: When to choose ReplaySubject over BehaviorSubject?
followUpsQA.a: When you need more than one past value replayed, or you have no sensible initial value.

followUpsQA.q: BehaviorSubject vs a Signal for state?
followUpsQA.a: Signals are synchronous and simpler for UI state; BehaviorSubject composes with RxJS operators.

followUpsQA.q: Does BehaviorSubject need an initial value?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, you must seed it with one.

questions.q: What is the difference between hot and cold Observables?

answer.oneLiner: Cold Observables create a new producer per subscriber (each subscribe re-runs the HTTP call); hot Observables share one producer among all subscribers (WebSocket, DOM events).
keyPoints: Cold — independent execution per subscriber (HttpClient.get)
Hot — shared stream (fromEvent, Subject, WebSocket)
`share()` / `shareReplay(1)` make a cold Observable hot
`shareReplay(1)` also caches the last value — good for HTTP
answer.detail: Subscribing twice to a cold HTTP Observable fires two requests; use `shareReplay(1)` to dedupe and cache. `Router.events` is hot; `HttpClient` is cold.

code.note: shareReplay(1) dedupes the request and caches the result

redFlags: Not knowing async pipe twice = two HTTP requests
Confusing multicasting with caching
Not knowing what shareReplay does

dialogue.text: What's the difference between hot and cold Observables?
Cold Observables create a new producer per subscriber, so each subscribe re-runs the work; hot Observables share one producer among all subscribers.
Give me an example of each in Angular.
`HttpClient.get` is cold, so two subscriptions fire two requests; `Router.events` or a Subject is hot and shared.
How would you stop a cold HTTP Observable firing twice?
Pipe it through `shareReplay(1)`, which makes it hot and caches the last value for late subscribers.

followUpsQA.q: share() vs shareReplay(1)?
followUpsQA.a: `share` multicasts live emissions; `shareReplay(1)` also caches and replays the last value to new subscribers.

followUpsQA.q: What happens with async pipe twice on the same cold http$?
followUpsQA.a: Two subscriptions means two HTTP requests unless you share it.

followUpsQA.q: How would you cache API calls with shareReplay?
followUpsQA.a: Apply `shareReplay(1)` once and reuse the same observable reference everywhere.

followUpsQA.q: Is multicasting the same as caching?
followUpsQA.a: No, multicasting shares a live stream; caching also replays a stored value.

questions.q: What are Angular Signals and how do they compare to RxJS for reactivity?

answer.oneLiner: Signals (v16+) are synchronous, fine-grained reactive values that auto-track dependencies; RxJS is better for async streams. Use signals for state, RxJS for async flows.
keyPoints: `signal(v)` — read `sig()`, write `.set()` / `.update()`
`computed()` — derived, lazily recomputed
`effect()` — side effects on signal change, auto-cleaned
Signals enable zoneless change detection
answer.detail: RxJS wins for async streams, time operators, and multicasting. Bridge with `toSignal(obs$)` and `toObservable(sig)`.

code.note: computed() derives reactively; read signals by calling them

redFlags: Thinking signals replace RxJS entirely
Not knowing toSignal/toObservable
Using effect() for state derivation instead of computed()

dialogue.text: What are signals and how do they compare to RxJS?
Signals are synchronous, fine-grained reactive values that auto-track their dependencies; RxJS is better for async streams. I use signals for state and RxJS for async flows.
How do you derive a value from a signal?
With `computed()`, which lazily recomputes only when a tracked signal changes.
Do they replace RxJS?
No; RxJS still wins for async, time-based, and multicast scenarios, and I bridge with `toSignal()` and `toObservable()`.

followUpsQA.q: signal.set() vs signal.update()?
followUpsQA.a: `set` replaces the value; `update` derives the new value from the current one.

followUpsQA.q: How does computed() avoid recomputation?
followUpsQA.a: It's lazy and memoised, recomputing only on read after a dependency changed.

followUpsQA.q: What is zoneless change detection?
followUpsQA.a: Running Angular without Zone.js, relying on signals and the async pipe to trigger updates.

followUpsQA.q: When should you use effect() vs computed()?
followUpsQA.a: Use computed for derived state, effect only for side effects.

questions.q: How does Angular change detection work? Explain Zone.js, dirty checking, and OnPush.

answer.oneLiner: Zone.js intercepts async events, then Angular walks the component tree top-down checking bindings. OnPush skips a component unless its input reference changes, it emits an event, or an async pipe emits.
keyPoints: Zone.js patches async APIs to trigger a CD cycle
Default CD checks every component each cycle
OnPush re-checks on input change, event, async emit, or markForCheck()
Signals (v16+) enable fine-grained, zoneless updates
answer.detail: OnPush needs immutable data — replace references, don't mutate. Use markForCheck() to flag a component dirty from outside the event flow (e.g. WebSocket push).

code.note: OnPush + signal input = minimal change detection overhead

redFlags: Not knowing what Zone.js does
Mutating data with OnPush and expecting updates
Using detach() without detectChanges()

dialogue.text: How does Angular change detection work?
Zone.js intercepts async events and triggers a cycle, then Angular walks the component tree top-down checking bindings; OnPush lets a component skip that unless something relevant changed.
What exactly triggers an OnPush component to re-check?
An input reference change, an event inside it, an async pipe emission, or an explicit `markForCheck()`.
So what's the gotcha with OnPush?
You must treat data as immutable; if you mutate an array in place the reference doesn't change, so OnPush won't pick it up.

followUpsQA.q: What if you mutate an array @Input on an OnPush component?
followUpsQA.a: The reference stays the same, so change detection skips it and the view goes stale.

followUpsQA.q: detectChanges() vs markForCheck()?
followUpsQA.a: `detectChanges` runs CD now for this view; `markForCheck` flags the path dirty for the next cycle.

followUpsQA.q: How do signals remove the need for Zone.js?
followUpsQA.a: Signal changes notify dependents directly, so Angular can update without Zone.js scanning everything.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use markForCheck()?
followUpsQA.a: For updates outside the event flow, like a WebSocket push into an OnPush component.

questions.q: What are Angular route guards? Explain CanActivate, CanDeactivate, CanActivateChild, and Resolve.

answer.oneLiner: Guards control navigation: CanActivate blocks entry, CanDeactivate prevents leaving, CanActivateChild guards children, and Resolve pre-fetches data before activation.
keyPoints: CanActivate — return true, false, or a UrlTree to redirect
CanDeactivate — 'unsaved changes' confirmation
Resolve — pre-fetch data before the route loads
CanMatch (v14+) — prevent a route matching at all
answer.detail: Modern Angular uses functional guards (plain functions with inject()) instead of classes. Multiple guards run sequentially; any false/UrlTree cancels navigation.

code.note: Factory function pattern to create parameterised guards

redFlags: Using class guards when functional is the modern way
Not knowing a UrlTree redirects
Using Resolve on every route

dialogue.text: Explain the route guards.
`CanActivate` blocks entering a route, `CanDeactivate` prevents leaving, `CanActivateChild` guards children, and `Resolve` pre-fetches data before activation.
How do you redirect instead of just blocking?
Return a `UrlTree` from the guard instead of `false`, and the router navigates there.
What's the modern way to write guards?
Functional guards, which are plain functions using `inject()`, instead of injectable classes.

followUpsQA.q: CanActivate vs CanMatch?
followUpsQA.a: CanActivate runs after a route matches; CanMatch decides whether the route config matches at all, useful for lazy loading.

followUpsQA.q: How to guard on an async condition?
followUpsQA.a: Return an Observable or Promise of boolean or UrlTree from the guard.

followUpsQA.q: Resolve vs fetching in ngOnInit, perf impact?
followUpsQA.a: Resolve delays navigation until data is ready; ngOnInit lets the route render then load with a skeleton.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if any guard returns false?
followUpsQA.a: Navigation is cancelled.

questions.q: What is the difference between switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, and exhaustMap?

answer.oneLiner: All map a value to an inner Observable and flatten it; they differ in concurrency: switchMap cancels the previous, concatMap queues, mergeMap runs in parallel, exhaustMap ignores new emissions while busy.
keyPoints: switchMap — cancels previous; use for type-ahead search
concatMap — sequential, preserves order; use for save queues
mergeMap — concurrent; use when order doesn't matter
exhaustMap — ignores new while busy; use for login buttons
answer.detail: Picking the wrong one causes classic bugs — duplicate requests, stale data, or lost clicks.

code.note: Each operator matched to its idiomatic use case

redFlags: Using mergeMap for type-ahead (stale, out-of-order)
Using switchMap for a save queue (cancels saves)
Not knowing these operators at senior level

dialogue.text: switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, exhaustMap, what's the difference?

They all map to an inner observable and flatten, but differ in concurrency: switchMap cancels the previous, concatMap queues in order, mergeMap runs in parallel, and exhaustMap ignores new emissions while busy.
Which for a type-ahead search box?
switchMap, so an outdated request is cancelled when the user keeps typing.
And for a login button you don't want double-firing?
exhaustMap, since it ignores extra clicks while the login request is in flight.

followUpsQA.q: What happens to an in-flight request when switchMap switches?
followUpsQA.a: It unsubscribes and cancels the previous inner observable.

followUpsQA.q: When does mergeMap break where concatMap works?
followUpsQA.a: When order matters, like sequential saves, mergeMap can interleave and corrupt order.

followUpsQA.q: How does exhaustMap prevent duplicate button clicks?
followUpsQA.a: It drops new outer values until the current inner observable completes.

followUpsQA.q: Which preserves order for a write queue?
followUpsQA.a: concatMap.

questions.q: What are Angular Signals and how do they work internally?

answer.oneLiner: Signals track read dependencies automatically; when a signal changes it marks only its dependent computeds and effects as dirty and updates them, no Zone.js needed.
keyPoints: Reading a signal in a reactive context registers it as a dependency
computed() re-runs only when a tracked signal changes
effect() runs eagerly and re-runs on any read signal change
Push-pull: change pushes 'dirty', consumers pull lazily
answer.detail: computed() is lazy — recomputes on read after a dependency changed. Expose read-only via `signal.asReadonly()`. v18+ adds `linkedSignal` and the `resource()` API for async data.

code.note: computed() lazily derives; effect() eagerly runs side effects on dependency change

redFlags: Thinking signals are just a BehaviorSubject wrapper
Using effect() to derive state instead of computed()
Not knowing computed() is lazy

dialogue.text: How do signals work internally?
Reading a signal in a reactive context registers it as a dependency; when it changes, it marks only its dependent computeds and effects dirty, no Zone.js involved.
Is computed eager or lazy?
Lazy; it only recomputes when you read it after a dependency changed, and the result is memoised.
How would you describe the propagation model?
Push-pull: a change pushes a dirty flag down the dependency graph, and consumers pull the recomputed value lazily on read.

followUpsQA.q: signal.set() vs signal.update()?
followUpsQA.a: `set` assigns a value; `update` computes the next value from the current one.

followUpsQA.q: Can you use signals outside components?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, signals work in services and plain functions; effects need an injection context or manual cleanup.

followUpsQA.q: What is linkedSignal?
followUpsQA.a: A writable signal that derives from a source but can be locally overridden, added in v18+.

followUpsQA.q: Are signals just a BehaviorSubject wrapper?
followUpsQA.a: No, they're a synchronous dependency-tracking primitive, not an RxJS construct.

questions.q: What is the trackBy function in ngFor/@for and why is it important for performance?

answer.oneLiner: trackBy gives each list item a stable identity so Angular reuses DOM nodes when the list changes instead of destroying and recreating everything.
keyPoints: Without it, a new array reference recreates all DOM nodes
trackBy returns a unique id (usually item.id)
Reuses nodes for unchanged ids, only updating properties
Angular 17 `@for` requires `track`
answer.detail: Critical when a list reloads from an API — same logical items get new object references. Big win for large lists and preserves child component state.

code.snippet:
• {{ item.name }}
    • @for (item of items; track item.id) {
  • {{ item.name }}
    • }
code.note: trackById is a class method returning item.id; @for makes track mandatory

redFlags: Not knowing what trackBy does
Tracking by index when items can reorder
Not knowing @for requires track

dialogue.text: What does trackBy do and why does it matter?
It gives each list item a stable identity, so when the array changes Angular reuses existing DOM nodes instead of destroying and recreating everything.
When does that really bite you?
When a list reloads from an API: the same logical items get brand-new object references, so without trackBy the whole list re-renders and child state is lost.
What changed in Angular 17's @for?
`track` is mandatory, so you can't accidentally omit it.

followUpsQA.q: What happens to child state on reload without trackBy?
followUpsQA.a: Child components are destroyed and recreated, losing local state like focus or input.

followUpsQA.q: When is tracking by index acceptable?
followUpsQA.a: For static lists that never reorder or get items inserted in the middle.

followUpsQA.q: How does required track in @for differ from optional trackBy?
followUpsQA.a: @for enforces track at compile time; trackBy in *ngFor is optional and easy to forget.

followUpsQA.q: What should trackBy return?
followUpsQA.a: A unique, stable identifier, usually item.id.

questions.q: What are pure and impure pipes in Angular?

answer.oneLiner: Pure pipes (default) only re-run when the input reference changes, so they're fast. Impure pipes run every change detection cycle — expensive but needed for mutable inputs.
keyPoints: Pure — transform() only on reference change; memoised
Impure (`pure: false`) — transform() every CD cycle
Pure pipes must be stateless and side-effect-free
The `async` pipe is impure
answer.detail: For mutable arrays, prefer returning a new reference and keeping the pipe pure over making it impure.

code.note: Pure pipe result is cached unless value or args change by reference

redFlags: Not knowing what the pure property does
Making filter/sort pipes impure without cost awareness
Not knowing async is impure

dialogue.text: What's the difference between pure and impure pipes?
Pure pipes, the default, only re-run when the input reference changes, so they're memoised and fast; impure pipes run on every change detection cycle.
Give me an impure pipe you use every day.
The `async` pipe is impure, because it has to react to emissions over time, not just reference changes.
What about a filter pipe over a mutable array?
Rather than making it impure, I'd return a new array reference and keep the pipe pure, since impure pipes run constantly.

followUpsQA.q: Why is the async pipe impure?
followUpsQA.a: It must re-evaluate as the underlying observable emits, not just on reference change.

followUpsQA.q: How would you make an impure filter pipe pure?
followUpsQA.a: Filter in the component and emit a new array reference so a pure pipe detects the change.

followUpsQA.q: Perf impact of many impure pipes?
followUpsQA.a: Each runs on every CD cycle, which can dominate frame time on large views.

followUpsQA.q: Must pure pipes be side-effect free?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, they must be stateless and deterministic.

questions.q: What are Angular HTTP Interceptors and what are common use cases?

answer.oneLiner: Interceptors are middleware that sit on every HttpClient request and response — used for auth tokens, error handling, logging, and caching without touching each service call.
keyPoints: Functional `HttpInterceptorFn` (v15+), registered via `withInterceptors([...])`
Intercept outgoing requests and incoming responses via `next`
Common: auth header, 401 handling, loading spinner, logging
Requests are immutable — modify with `req.clone({...})`
answer.detail: Requests pass through interceptors in order; responses come back in reverse.

code.note: Clone the immutable request to add a header

redFlags: Not knowing requests are immutable and must be cloned
Not knowing the functional interceptor syntax
Adding auth headers in every service call manually

dialogue.text: What are HTTP interceptors used for?
They're middleware on every HttpClient request and response, used for auth tokens, error handling, logging, and caching without touching each service call.
How do you modify an outgoing request?
Requests are immutable, so I clone them with `req.clone({...})` to add headers, then pass it to `next`.
Does order matter when you register several?
Yes; requests flow through them in registration order and responses come back in reverse.

followUpsQA.q: How to skip an interceptor for a specific request?
followUpsQA.a: Use an HttpContext token and check it inside the interceptor to bypass logic.

followUpsQA.q: How to queue requests during a token refresh?
followUpsQA.a: Catch the 401, refresh once, and use a BehaviorSubject to hold and retry queued requests.

followUpsQA.q: Does interceptor order matter?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, requests run in order and responses unwind in reverse.

followUpsQA.q: Why must you clone the request?
followUpsQA.a: HttpRequest is immutable, so direct mutation isn't allowed.

questions.q: What are Deferrable Views (@defer) in Angular 17?

answer.oneLiner: @defer is template syntax that lazily loads a component on a trigger (viewport, interaction, idle, etc.), improving initial bundle size and Core Web Vitals.
keyPoints: Triggers: `on viewport`, `on idle`, `on interaction`, `on hover`, `on timer`, `when`
`@placeholder` before load, `@loading` during, `@error` on failure
The chunk is code-split automatically by the compiler
Works at template level, not just route level
answer.detail: `prefetch` loads the chunk in the background while showing on a different trigger, e.g. `prefetch on idle` but render `on viewport`.

code.note: Shows a button; prefetches the chunk when browser is idle; renders comments on click

redFlags: Not knowing @defer as a senior dev
Thinking lazy loading is route-only
Confusing @placeholder with @loading

dialogue.text: What are deferrable views with @defer?
It's template syntax that lazily loads a block of UI on a trigger like viewport, interaction, or idle, which cuts the initial bundle and improves Core Web Vitals.
What sub-blocks does it support?
`@placeholder` before load, `@loading` while loading, and `@error` if it fails.
How does it differ from lazy routes?
It works at the template level on any block, not just whole routes, and the compiler code-splits the chunk automatically.

followUpsQA.q: How does @defer differ from lazy routes?
followUpsQA.a: It defers any template block, while lazy routes defer entire routed components.

followUpsQA.q: Can @defer depend on auth state?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, use the `when` trigger with a boolean condition.

followUpsQA.q: @placeholder vs @loading?
followUpsQA.a: `@placeholder` shows before loading starts; `@loading` shows during the fetch.

followUpsQA.q: What does prefetch do?
followUpsQA.a: It loads the chunk in the background on one trigger while rendering on another.

questions.q: What are Route Resolvers in Angular and when should you use them?

answer.oneLiner: A Resolver pre-fetches data before a route activates, so the data is ready in the component immediately — no in-component loading state.
keyPoints: Completes before the component mounts — no spinner inside it
Modern syntax: `ResolveFn<T>` with inject()
Read via `ActivatedRoute.snapshot.data['key']`
Best for data essential to render (e.g. edit page)
answer.detail: Trade-off: navigation feels slower (router waits) vs a component-level skeleton. For most apps, fetching in the component with a skeleton gives better perceived UX.

code.note: Return EMPTY to cancel navigation on error

redFlags: Using resolvers for optional sidebar content
Not handling resolver errors (navigation hangs)
Accessing route data with the wrong key

dialogue.text: What's a route resolver and when do you use it?
It pre-fetches data before a route activates, so the component has its data ready immediately with no internal loading state.
How does the component read that data?
From `ActivatedRoute`, usually `snapshot.data['key']` or the `data` observable.
Any downside?
Navigation feels slower because the router waits, so for most pages a component skeleton gives better perceived UX; I reserve resolvers for data that's essential to render.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if a resolver returns EMPTY or throws?
followUpsQA.a: Returning EMPTY cancels navigation; an unhandled error can leave navigation hanging, so handle it.

followUpsQA.q: How to pass resolver data to child routes?
followUpsQA.a: Child routes can read parent route data via the ActivatedRoute hierarchy.

followUpsQA.q: Resolver vs ngOnInit fetch, UX trade-off?
followUpsQA.a: Resolver delays the page until ready; ngOnInit renders fast with a skeleton.

followUpsQA.q: What's the modern resolver syntax?
followUpsQA.a: A functional `ResolveFn<T>` using `inject()`.

questions.q: How does Angular's ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush optimization work in practice?

answer.oneLiner: OnPush skips a component's change detection unless its @Input reference changes, it emits an event, an async pipe emits, or markForCheck() is called — a big win for large trees.
keyPoints: Re-checks on: input ref change, event, async emit, markForCheck()
Requires immutability — return new references, don't mutate
markForCheck() for outside-the-event updates (WebSocket)
Combine with signal inputs for zoneless performance
answer.detail: `this.items = [...this.items, x]` updates; `this.items.push(x)` does not. Signals propagate changes without manual markForCheck().

code.note: Immutable updates trigger OnPush; mutations don't

redFlags: Mutating data and expecting OnPush to detect it
Not knowing markForCheck vs detectChanges
Not realising async pipe calls markForCheck

dialogue.text: How does OnPush work in practice?
It skips a component's change detection unless its input reference changes, it emits an event, an async pipe emits, or `markForCheck()` is called.
Show me an update that works and one that breaks it.
`this.items = [...this.items, x]` works because it's a new reference; `this.items.push(x)` breaks it because the reference is unchanged.
How do you handle a WebSocket push into an OnPush component?
Update the field and call `markForCheck()`, or pass the data as a signal so it propagates automatically.

followUpsQA.q: markForCheck() vs detectChanges()?
followUpsQA.a: `markForCheck` schedules a check up the tree for next cycle; `detectChanges` runs CD synchronously now.

followUpsQA.q: OnPush with data from a service (not @Input)?
followUpsQA.a: Use the async pipe or signals, or call markForCheck on updates.

followUpsQA.q: Risks of ChangeDetectorRef.detach()?
followUpsQA.a: The view stops updating entirely until you reattach or call detectChanges manually.

followUpsQA.q: Does the async pipe trigger OnPush?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, it calls markForCheck on each emission.

questions.q: What are Angular Schematics and how does the CLI use them?

answer.oneLiner: Schematics are code-transformation scripts the CLI runs to scaffold files, modify code, and migrate APIs — behind ng generate, ng add, and ng update.
keyPoints: Functions that take a virtual `Tree` and return a modified one
`ng generate` runs built-in schematics; `ng add` runs a library's
`ng update` runs migration schematics across major versions
Custom schematics enforce team architecture patterns
answer.detail: Changes are atomic — only written if the whole schematic succeeds. Schematics can do AST manipulation (add imports, edit decorators).
redFlags: Not knowing what runs behind ng generate/ng add
Updating Angular without ng update (misses migrations)

dialogue.text: What are Angular schematics?
They're code-transformation scripts the CLI runs to scaffold files, modify code, and migrate APIs; they power `ng generate`, `ng add`, and `ng update`.
How do they actually work?
A schematic is a function that takes a virtual file Tree and returns a modified Tree, applied atomically so nothing is written unless the whole thing succeeds.
Why does ng update matter beyond bumping a version?
It runs migration schematics that automatically update your code for breaking API changes across major versions.

followUpsQA.q: How would you write a custom team schematic?
followUpsQA.a: Author a Rule that manipulates the Tree, optionally with AST edits, and publish it as a collection.

followUpsQA.q: ng add vs ng install?
followUpsQA.a: `ng add` installs and runs the package's setup schematic; plain install just adds the dependency.

followUpsQA.q: How do update schematics differ from a package bump?
followUpsQA.a: They rewrite your code for breaking changes, not just change the version number.

followUpsQA.q: Are schematic changes atomic?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, the Tree is only committed if the whole schematic succeeds.

questions.q: How would you optimise a slow Angular application? What tools and techniques would you use?

answer.oneLiner: Profile first (Angular DevTools, bundle analyser), then apply OnPush/signals, lazy load and @defer, use track in loops, prefer pure pipes over template methods, and trim the bundle.
keyPoints: Profile first — Angular DevTools, Chrome Performance, source-map-explorer
Change detection — OnPush + immutability, or signals for zoneless
Bundle — lazy routes, @defer, avoid huge dependencies
Templates — track in loops, pure pipes, no function calls in templates
answer.detail: Manage subscriptions with takeUntilDestroyed/async pipe. For Core Web Vitals, add SSR (`ng add @angular/ssr`) for LCP and @defer to cut TBT.

code.snippet:
• {{ formatName(i) }}
    • @for (i of items(); track i.id) {
  • {{ i.name | titlecase }}
    • }
code.note: Avoid method calls in templates; use signals/pure pipes with track

redFlags: Optimising without profiling first
Applying OnPush everywhere without immutability
Not knowing DevTools or bundle analyser

dialogue.text: How would you optimise a slow Angular app?
Profile first with Angular DevTools and a bundle analyser, then apply OnPush or signals, lazy load with routes and `@defer`, use track in loops, prefer pure pipes, and trim the bundle.
Why profile before changing anything?
So I fix the real bottleneck instead of guessing; the slowdown might be change detection, a huge dependency, or a chatty template.
One template anti-pattern you'd hunt for?
Method calls in templates, since they re-run every change detection cycle; I'd switch to a signal or a pure pipe.

followUpsQA.q: How to find which component causes excessive CD?
followUpsQA.a: Use Angular DevTools' profiler flame chart to see CD time per component.

followUpsQA.q: Method in template vs pure pipe, impact?
followUpsQA.a: A method runs every CD cycle; a pure pipe is memoised and only re-runs on input change.

followUpsQA.q: How does SSR improve performance metrics?
followUpsQA.a: It serves rendered HTML for faster LCP and better perceived load.

followUpsQA.q: What cuts the initial bundle most?
followUpsQA.a: Lazy routes and `@defer` blocks plus removing heavy dependencies.

questions.q: What is ViewEncapsulation (Emulated, None, ShadowDom), and how does viewProviders differ from it?

answer.oneLiner: ViewEncapsulation controls how a component's CSS is scoped; viewProviders is unrelated — it scopes DI providers to the component's view only (not its projected content).
keyPoints: **Emulated** (default) — Angular adds attributes to scope CSS to the component
**None** — styles are global, leak everywhere
**ShadowDom** — uses native Shadow DOM for true isolation
`viewProviders` — DI scoping (services for the view, not ng-content children), NOT styling

code.note: encapsulation = style scoping; viewProviders = DI scoping — different concerns.

redFlags: Conflating viewProviders with style encapsulation
Using None and wondering why styles leak

dialogue.text: Explain ViewEncapsulation and how viewProviders differs.
ViewEncapsulation controls how a component's CSS is scoped; viewProviders is unrelated and scopes DI providers to the component's view only, not its projected content.
What are the encapsulation modes?
Emulated, the default, scopes styles with generated attributes; None makes them global; ShadowDom uses native Shadow DOM for true isolation.
So what does viewProviders actually do?
It provides a service to the component's own view but withholds it from content projected in through `<ng-content>`.

followUpsQA.q: Emulated vs ShadowDom trade-offs?
followUpsQA.a: Emulated is portable and works everywhere; ShadowDom gives real isolation but limits global style theming.

followUpsQA.q: providers vs viewProviders?
followUpsQA.a: `providers` is visible to the view and projected content; `viewProviders` only to the view.

followUpsQA.q: Why do styles leak with None?
followUpsQA.a: Because Angular doesn't scope them, so they become global CSS.

followUpsQA.q: Is viewProviders about styling?
followUpsQA.a: No, it's purely DI scoping.

questions.q: How do you write a custom directive, and what makes a directive structural vs attribute?

answer.oneLiner: A directive is an @Directive class with a CSS selector; attribute directives change an element via ElementRef/HostBinding, while structural directives inject TemplateRef + ViewContainerRef to add/remove DOM.
keyPoints: Selector in `[]` makes it an attribute selector (e.g. `[appHighlight]`)
Attribute directive: manipulate the host element
Structural directive: inject `TemplateRef` and `ViewContainerRef`
The `*` prefix is sugar that wraps the element in an `<ng-template>`

code.note: Structural directive: TemplateRef + ViewContainerRef to conditionally render.

redFlags: Not knowing structural directives use TemplateRef/ViewContainerRef
Confusing the * sugar

dialogue.text: How do you write a custom directive?
Create an `@Directive` class with a CSS attribute selector; an attribute directive changes the host via ElementRef or HostBinding, while a structural one injects `TemplateRef` and `ViewContainerRef`.
So what makes it structural specifically?
It uses `TemplateRef` and `ViewContainerRef` to add or remove DOM, like building your own `*ngIf`.
What's the asterisk doing for a structural directive?
It's sugar that wraps the host element in an `<ng-template>` and passes the directive input to it.

followUpsQA.q: Why only one structural directive per element?
followUpsQA.a: Each needs its own template wrapper, so they'd conflict; use ng-container to combine.

followUpsQA.q: How does *appUnless expand?
followUpsQA.a: Into an `<ng-template [appUnless]="...">` wrapping the element.

followUpsQA.q: What does an attribute directive inject?
followUpsQA.a: Typically ElementRef and uses HostBinding/HostListener to affect the host.

followUpsQA.q: What selector form marks an attribute directive?
followUpsQA.a: A bracketed selector like `[appHighlight]`.

questions.q: What are @HostListener and @HostBinding used for?

answer.oneLiner: @HostListener subscribes to events on the host element; @HostBinding binds a property/attribute/class/style of the host element — both commonly used inside directives.
keyPoints: `@HostListener('click', ['$event'])` — react to host events
`@HostBinding('class.active')` — drive host class/style/attr reactively
Avoids manual ElementRef/Renderer wiring
Idiomatic way to build interactive attribute directives

code.note: HostListener reacts to events; HostBinding reflects state onto the host.

redFlags: Manipulating the DOM directly instead of HostBinding
Not knowing they work in directives

dialogue.text: What are @HostListener and @HostBinding for?
`@HostListener` subscribes to events on the host element, and `@HostBinding` binds a property, class, or style of the host; both are common inside directives.
Why use them instead of ElementRef and Renderer2?
They're declarative and avoid manual DOM wiring, which is cleaner and integrates with change detection.
How would you build a hover-highlight directive?
HostListener for `mouseenter`/`mouseleave` to flip a flag, and HostBinding on `class.hovered` to reflect it.

followUpsQA.q: HostBinding vs Renderer2?
followUpsQA.a: HostBinding is declarative for the host; Renderer2 is imperative and works on any element safely across platforms.

followUpsQA.q: How to pass $event to a HostListener?
followUpsQA.a: Add `['$event']` as the second argument, like `@HostListener('click', ['$event'])`.

followUpsQA.q: Can these be used outside directives?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, they also work in components since a component is a directive.

followUpsQA.q: Why prefer HostBinding over direct DOM writes?
followUpsQA.a: It stays in Angular's rendering pipeline and is SSR-safe.

questions.q: How do you create a component manually, without the Angular CLI?

answer.oneLiner: Write a TypeScript class, decorate it with @Component (selector, template/templateUrl, styles), make it standalone or declare it in a module, then use its selector in a parent template.
keyPoints: Create a `.ts` file with an exported class
Add `@Component({ selector, standalone: true, template })`
Import it in the parent's `imports: [...]` (standalone)
Reference it via its selector tag in the parent template

code.note: No CLI needed — the @Component decorator is all that defines a component.

redFlags: Thinking the CLI is required to make a component
Forgetting to import a standalone component

dialogue.text: How would you create a component without the CLI?
Write a TypeScript class, decorate it with `@Component` giving a selector, template, and styles, mark it standalone, then use its selector in a parent.
How does the parent get to use it?
For a standalone component, add it to the parent's `imports` array, then reference it by its selector tag.
So is the CLI strictly necessary?
No, the `@Component` decorator is all that defines a component; the CLI just saves you the boilerplate and wiring.

followUpsQA.q: What does the CLI actually generate beyond this?
followUpsQA.a: The class, template, styles, a spec file, and any module or import wiring.

followUpsQA.q: How to register it without standalone?
followUpsQA.a: Declare it in an NgModule's `declarations` and export it if other modules need it.

followUpsQA.q: What's the minimum a component needs?
followUpsQA.a: A class with `@Component` specifying at least a template.

followUpsQA.q: Why might you forget to import a standalone component?
followUpsQA.a: There's no module declaration, so it must be in the consumer's imports or it won't render.

questions.q: How do you scope styles to a particular component, and what does encapsulation guarantee?

answer.oneLiner: Put styles in the component's `styles`/`styleUrls`; with default Emulated encapsulation Angular rewrites selectors with a unique attribute so they only apply to that component's own elements.
keyPoints: Component styles are scoped to the component by default (Emulated)
`:host` targets the component's own host element
`:host-context(...)` reacts to ancestor classes
`::ng-deep` (deprecated) pierces into children — avoid

code.note: .title here won't affect any other component's .title elements.

redFlags: Using ::ng-deep as the default tool
Not knowing styles are scoped by default

dialogue.text: How do you scope styles to a component?
Put them in the component's `styles` or `styleUrls`; with the default Emulated encapsulation Angular rewrites selectors with a unique attribute so they only hit that component's elements.
How do you style the component's own root element?
With the `:host` selector, and `:host-context(...)` to react to an ancestor class.
What about reaching into a child's styles?
I avoid `::ng-deep` since it's deprecated; I'd expose CSS custom properties or restructure instead.

followUpsQA.q: What does :host do?
followUpsQA.a: It targets the component's own host element from inside its styles.

followUpsQA.q: Why avoid ::ng-deep?
followUpsQA.a: It's deprecated and breaks encapsulation by leaking styles into children.

followUpsQA.q: Are component styles scoped by default?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, Emulated encapsulation scopes them automatically.

followUpsQA.q: How do you intentionally make a style global?
followUpsQA.a: Use ViewEncapsulation.None or put it in global styles.css.

questions.q: Why shouldn't the constructor do heavy work, and what belongs in ngOnChanges instead?

answer.oneLiner: The constructor runs before @Inputs are set and is for DI only; logic that reacts to inputs belongs in ngOnChanges (every input change) or ngOnInit (after first inputs are bound).
keyPoints: Constructor: @Inputs are still undefined — only inject dependencies
`ngOnChanges` — runs on every @Input change with the old/new values
`ngOnInit` — runs once after the first inputs are set
Heavy/async work in the constructor hurts testability and timing

code.note: Inputs are guaranteed set in ngOnChanges/ngOnInit, never in the constructor.

redFlags: Reading @Input values in the constructor
Doing HTTP in the constructor

dialogue.text: Why shouldn't the constructor do heavy work?
Because it runs before inputs are set and is really only for dependency injection; reading inputs there gives undefined and heavy or async work hurts testability and timing.
So where does input-reactive logic go?
In `ngOnChanges`, which fires on every input change with old and new values, or `ngOnInit` after the first inputs are bound.
Quick example of something that belongs in ngOnChanges?
Reloading a user when an `@Input() userId` changes, since the constructor wouldn't see the new value.

followUpsQA.q: Why are @Inputs undefined in the constructor?
followUpsQA.a: Inputs are set during binding, which happens after construction.

followUpsQA.q: ngOnChanges vs ngOnInit?
followUpsQA.a: ngOnChanges runs on every input change; ngOnInit runs once after the first inputs are set.

followUpsQA.q: Is doing HTTP in the constructor a problem?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, it couples construction to side effects and hurts testability; do it in ngOnInit.

followUpsQA.q: What is the constructor good for?
followUpsQA.a: Injecting dependencies and simple field initialization.

questions.q: How do you restrict a service so it has a single instance per component (component-level providers)?

answer.oneLiner: Add the service to the component's `providers: []` array — Angular's hierarchical injector then creates a fresh instance per component instance, destroyed with the component.
keyPoints: `providedIn: 'root'` = app-wide singleton; component `providers` = per-component
Each instance of the component gets its own service instance
Instance is destroyed when the component is destroyed
Good for per-component state isolation

code.note: Component-level providers scope and isolate the service to that component subtree.

redFlags: Thinking providedIn:'root' can be per-component
Not knowing the hierarchical injector

dialogue.text: How do you give a service a single instance per component?
Add it to the component's `providers` array; the hierarchical injector then creates a fresh instance per component instance.
When is that instance destroyed?
When the component is destroyed, so it's perfect for per-component state isolation.
How does that differ from providedIn: 'root'?
`providedIn: 'root'` is one shared singleton for the whole app, whereas component providers give a separate instance per component.

followUpsQA.q: providers vs viewProviders?
followUpsQA.a: `providers` is visible to the view and projected content; `viewProviders` only to the view.

followUpsQA.q: What happens to the instance on destroy?
followUpsQA.a: It's torn down with the component, and ngOnDestroy on the service runs.

followUpsQA.q: Can providedIn: 'root' be per-component?
followUpsQA.a: No, it's always an app-wide singleton.

followUpsQA.q: Why use a component-scoped service?
followUpsQA.a: To isolate state so each component instance has its own.

questions.q: How do you preserve form data when the user navigates away and comes back?

answer.oneLiner: Lift the form state out of the component into a shared service (or a store), and optionally use a CanDeactivate guard to warn before discarding unsaved changes.
keyPoints: Store form values in a singleton service so they survive component destruction
