Rehydrate the form from the service in ngOnInit
`CanDeactivate` guard — confirm before leaving with unsaved changes
For persistence across reloads, use sessionStorage/localStorage

code.snippet:
export const unsavedGuard: CanDeactivateFn<FormComponent> =
  (cmp) => cmp.form.pristine || confirm('Discard unsaved changes?');
code.note: Guard prevents accidental data loss; a service preserves data on intentional navigation.

redFlags: Keeping all state inside the component (lost on destroy)
No warning before discarding edits

dialogue.text: How would you preserve form data when a user navigates away and back?
Lift the form state out of the component into a shared service or store, so it survives the component being destroyed, and rehydrate the form in ngOnInit.
What about warning them before they lose unsaved edits?
A `CanDeactivate` guard that checks if the form is dirty and confirms before leaving.
And surviving a full page reload?
Persist to sessionStorage or localStorage and restore on init.

followUpsQA.q: Service state vs sessionStorage trade-off?
followUpsQA.a: Service state survives navigation within the app; storage also survives reloads but needs serialization.

followUpsQA.q: How does CanDeactivate get the component?
followUpsQA.a: The router passes the component instance as the first argument to the guard.

followUpsQA.q: Where do you rehydrate the form?
followUpsQA.a: In ngOnInit from the service or storage.

followUpsQA.q: What's the risk of keeping all state in the component?
followUpsQA.a: It's lost when the component is destroyed on navigation.

questions.q: What is Module Federation / micro-frontends, and when would you use them?

answer.oneLiner: Micro-frontends split a UI into independently built and deployed apps; Module Federation (webpack) lets one app load another's code at runtime, even sharing dependencies.
keyPoints: Independent teams own, build, and deploy their slice separately
Module Federation: a host loads remote chunks at runtime
Shared deps (Angular, React) deduped via the `shared` config
Use when scaling many teams; overkill for a small single-team app

code.note: Host imports remote modules at runtime; shared singletons avoid duplicate framework loads.

redFlags: Adopting micro-frontends for a tiny single-team app
Not knowing about shared deps

dialogue.text: What are micro-frontends and Module Federation?
Micro-frontends split a UI into independently built and deployed apps; Module Federation, a webpack feature, lets a host app load another app's code at runtime and share dependencies.
How are shared frameworks handled so you don't ship Angular twice?
You list them in the `shared` config so the host and remotes dedupe them to a single shared singleton.
When would you avoid this architecture?
For a small single-team app, where the operational complexity outweighs the benefit; it really pays off when many teams ship independently.

followUpsQA.q: What are the downsides of micro-frontends?
followUpsQA.a: Operational complexity, version skew across teams, and harder cross-app consistency.

followUpsQA.q: How are shared dependencies handled?
followUpsQA.a: Via the `shared` config that dedupes common libs into singletons.

followUpsQA.q: What is a host vs a remote?
followUpsQA.a: The host loads and orchestrates remotes, which expose modules to be consumed at runtime.

followUpsQA.q: Is Module Federation tied to webpack?
followUpsQA.a: It originated in webpack 5, though Native Federation brings the concept to esbuild builds.

questions.q: What is the virtual DOM in React, and how does it differ from Angular's approach?

answer.oneLiner: React keeps an in-memory virtual DOM tree, diffs the new tree against the old (reconciliation), and patches only the changed real DOM nodes; Angular uses incremental DOM with no virtual tree.
keyPoints: VDOM = lightweight JS representation of the UI
On state change, React renders a new VDOM and diffs it
Only the minimal real-DOM changes are applied
Angular's Ivy compiles templates to instructions — no intermediate tree
redFlags: Saying the VDOM is always faster than the real DOM
Not knowing Angular has no VDOM

dialogue.text: What's React's virtual DOM and how does Angular differ?
React keeps an in-memory virtual DOM, diffs the new tree against the old in reconciliation, and patches only the changed real nodes; Angular uses incremental DOM with no virtual tree.
What does reconciliation buy React?
It minimises real-DOM mutations by computing the smallest set of changes from the diff.
So how does Angular avoid a virtual tree?
Ivy compiles each template into instructions that update specific nodes directly, so there's no intermediate tree to build or diff.

followUpsQA.q: What is reconciliation?
followUpsQA.a: React's process of diffing the new virtual DOM against the previous one to find minimal real-DOM updates.

followUpsQA.q: Why do keys matter in React lists?
followUpsQA.a: They give items stable identity so React reuses nodes instead of re-rendering, like Angular's trackBy.

followUpsQA.q: Does Angular have a virtual DOM?
followUpsQA.a: No, it uses incremental DOM via Ivy.

followUpsQA.q: Is the virtual DOM always faster?
followUpsQA.a: No, it's a strategy with overhead; both approaches perform well in practice.

questions.q: What are useMemo and useRef in React, and when do you use each?

answer.oneLiner: useMemo caches an expensive computed value between renders until its dependencies change; useRef holds a mutable value (or a DOM node) that persists across renders without causing re-renders.
keyPoints: `useMemo(fn, deps)` — memoise a costly calculation
`useRef(initial)` — mutable `.current` that survives renders, no re-render on change
useRef is also how you grab a DOM element
Don't overuse useMemo — it has its own cost

code.note: useMemo recomputes only when deps change; useRef.current mutates silently.

redFlags: Using state for a value that shouldn't trigger renders
Memoising trivial computations

dialogue.text: What are useMemo and useRef, and when do you use each?
`useMemo` caches an expensive computed value between renders until its deps change; `useRef` holds a mutable value or DOM node that persists across renders without triggering a re-render.
Why doesn't changing a ref re-render the component?
Because `useRef` writes to `.current` outside React's state system, so React doesn't schedule a render.
Any caution on useMemo?
Don't overuse it; memoisation has its own cost, so it's only worth it for genuinely expensive work.

followUpsQA.q: useMemo vs useCallback?
followUpsQA.a: `useMemo` memoises a value; `useCallback` memoises a function reference.

followUpsQA.q: Why doesn't updating a ref re-render?
followUpsQA.a: Ref writes bypass state, so no render is scheduled.

followUpsQA.q: What's the Angular analogue of useMemo?
followUpsQA.a: A `computed()` signal, which memoises a derived value.

followUpsQA.q: How do you grab a DOM node with a ref?
followUpsQA.a: Attach the ref to an element and read `ref.current`.

questions.q: What is React's Context API, and how does it solve deep prop passing?

answer.oneLiner: Context lets you provide a value at a high level and consume it anywhere below without threading props through every intermediate component — it solves prop drilling.
keyPoints: `createContext` → `<Provider value>` → `useContext` to read
Avoids passing props through components that don't need them
Good for theme, auth, locale — relatively stable values
Frequent context changes re-render all consumers (perf caveat)

code.note: Conceptually similar to an Angular service provided high in the injector tree.

redFlags: Using Context for high-frequency updates
Not knowing it solves prop drilling

dialogue.text: What's React's Context API and what problem does it solve?
Context lets you provide a value high in the tree and consume it anywhere below with `useContext`, so it solves prop drilling through components that don't need the data.
What kind of values suit Context?
Relatively stable cross-cutting values like theme, auth, or locale.
What's the performance caveat?
When the context value changes, all consumers re-render, so it's a poor fit for high-frequency updates.

followUpsQA.q: When use a state library over Context?
followUpsQA.a: For frequent updates or complex global state where selective subscriptions matter.

followUpsQA.q: Why can Context hurt performance?
followUpsQA.a: Every consumer re-renders on any value change.

followUpsQA.q: What's the Angular analogue?
followUpsQA.a: A service provided high in the injector tree and injected where needed.

followUpsQA.q: What three pieces make up Context?
followUpsQA.a: `createContext`, a `Provider`, and `useContext` to read.
